coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of watercress-derived N,S-doped carbon dots for sensitive spectrofluorimetric determination of rifaximin antibiotic: Greenness, blueness, and whiteness assessment

coredata.dc:description: A rapid microwave-assisted method was developed for synthesizing watercress-derived nitrogen and sulfur carbon dots (N,S-CDs) in just one minute using thiourea as a nitrogen and sulfur source and watercress as a green carbon source. Different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques were used to characterize the N,S-CDs. The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 386 nm after being excited at 320 nm. Rifaximin (RFX) could quench the native fluorescence of the N,S-CDs quantitatively based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching mechanisms. The results revealed a good linearity over the range of 0.2–30.0 µg/mL, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9996. The developed method demonstrated excellent accuracy, with recovery values between 99.16 % and 99.97 %, and good precision, as the %RSD values were ≤ 1.3 % for both intra-day and inter-day precision. The detection and quantitation limits were found to be 0.06 µg/mL and 0.19 µg/mL, respectively. The method was successfully applied for determining RFX in its tablets with high selectivity. The developed method produced comparable results to those obtained by the published HPLC method in terms of precision and accuracy. Finally, the developed method demonstrated excellent green, blue, and white characteristics based on the results obtained via the analytical Eco-scale, AGREE, MoGAPI, BAGI, and RGB12 tools. The suggested method offers a sensitive, fast, and eco-friendly spectrofluorimetric methodology for estimating the RFX without requiring hazardous organic solvents or complicated equipment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A rapid microwave-assisted method was developed for synthesizing watercress-derived nitrogen and sulfur carbon dots (N,S-CDs) in just one minute using thiourea as a nitrogen and sulfur source and watercress as a green carbon source. Different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques were used to characterize the N,S-CDs. The synthesized N,S-CDs exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 386 nm after being excited at 320 nm. Rifaximin (RFX) could quench the native fluorescence of the N,S-CDs quantitatively based on the inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching mechanisms. The results revealed a good linearity over the range of 0.2–30.0 µg/mL, with a correlation coefficient of 0.9996. The developed method demonstrated excellent accuracy, with recovery values between 99.16 % and 99.97 %, and good precision, as the %RSD values were ≤ 1.3 % for both intra-day and inter-day precision. The detection and quantitation limits were found to be 0.06 µg/mL and 0.19 µg/mL, respectively. The method was successfully applied for determining RFX in its tablets with high selectivity. The developed method produced comparable results to those obtained by the published HPLC method in terms of precision and accuracy. Finally, the developed method demonstrated excellent green, blue, and white characteristics based on the results obtained via the analytical Eco-scale, AGREE, MoGAPI, BAGI, and RGB12 tools. The suggested method offers a sensitive, fast, and eco-friendly spectrofluorimetric methodology for estimating the RFX without requiring hazardous organic solvents or complicated equipment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are a class of antimicrobial substances used to treat, prevent, and enhance growth in human medicine, aquaculture, and the livestock industry. Antibiotics not only treat infectious diseases, but also enable many contemporary medical procedures, such as open heart surgery, organ transplantation, and cancer treatment [1]. On the other hand, antibiotic misuse may allow antibiotics to enter the environment through land and water, causing a variety of harmful effects, and can also lead to a sharp increase in antimicrobial resistance, making certain infections incurable in practice [2]. Therefore, the development of new antimicrobial agents, such as rifaximin, is needed because of the increase in antibiotic resistance worldwide. Rifaximin (RFX) is a non-systemic and nonabsorbable oral antibiotic derived from rifamycin [3,4]. It is a benzimidazole derivative utilized for the treatment of different diseases, such as Clostridium difficile colitis, ulcerative colitis, Helicobacter pylori gastritis, pediatric bacterial diarrhea, irritable bowel syndrome, and hepatic encephalopathy. It is also commonly prescribed to patients with noninvasive Escherichia coli strains causing traveler diarrhea [5]. Bacterial RNA synthesis is inhibited by RFX, which binds to the beta-subunit of the DNA-dependent RNA polymerase and blocks the polymerization of deoxyribonucleotides into a DNA strand. Studies conducted in vitro on RFX have shown that it has wide-spectrum activity against anaerobic, gram-positive, and gram-negative bacteria, as well as there is little chance of bacterial resistance developing [6]. Several techniques, such as spectrophotometric [7–9], spectrofluorimetric [10], infrared spectroscopic [11], electrochemistry [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], and chromatographic methods [14–17], have been used to determine RFX in various matrices. The majority of these techniques are used to determine RFX in dried blood spots, mouse serum, human plasma, and rat urine. Regrettably, the majority of these reported methodologies do not consider the green analytical chemistry principles. These methods include the use of hazardous chemicals that can harm both the environment and the operator, costly tools and accessories, upkeep and regular training, reagent preparation time, analysis time, and the cost of disposal and waste treatment. As a result, it is very important to develop an eco-friendly, green, and fast method for determining RFX in its pharmaceutical dosage forms. Compared with other techniques, spectrofluorimetry is a technique that offers quick measurements and exhibits greater selectivity and sensitivity than UV/visible spectroscopy [18–20]. Unlike chromatographic techniques, conditioning and preparation of a mobile phase are not needed. Moreover, its environmental friendliness and applicability are increased because it eliminates the need for expensive instrumentation and excessive amounts of organic solvents [21–26].

Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconductor carbon nanomaterials with special electrical, thermal, and optical properties. They show atoms with different energy levels whose band gaps vary with size [27,28]. Since the quantum confinement depends on the size of the material, there are several techniques to construct therapeutic QDs. Traditional QDs are composed of group II-IV elements such as cadmium selenide (CdSe), cadmium telluride (CdTe), zinc sulfide (ZnS), zinc selenide (ZnSe), and zinc sulfide (ZnS), as well as group III-V elements such as gallium arsenate, gallium nitride (GaN), indium phosphate (InP), and indium arsenate (InAs). QDs with increased fluorescence, size-dependent multi-fluorescent activity, and resistance to photobleaching have recently been produced by many studies. They have been used in a variety of biological and pharmaceutical applications, including drug delivery, photodynamic treatment, photovoltaic devices, biosensors, biolabels, cellular tracking, in vivo optical imaging, and catalysis. Despite the exceptional optical and electrical properties of QDs, a variety of health problems, such as neurotoxicity, loss of cellular activity, carcinogenesis, and cell injury, limit their use in different fields, including biomedical and analytical applications. Some disadvantages of semiconductor inorganic quantum dots include their high toxicity, which is caused by the use of heavy metals in their manufacture. Carbon dots (CDs) were developed as a replacement for inorganic quantum dots due to their similar fluorescence properties, minimal toxicity, and chemical inertness, which allowed for their application in biomedical and pharmaceutical fields [29,30].

CDs are a class of carbon nanomaterials that have garnered significant interest due to their great biocompatibility, ease of synthesis, high photostability, and affordability [27,31–33]. CDs are promising materials for different applications, including sensors, bioimaging, drug delivery, photovoltaics, and photocatalysis [33]. Other uses of CDs include fluorescence sensors for detecting metal ions, pharmaceuticals, chemical compounds, as well as macromolecules. Generally, the use of CDs as a fluorescence sensor is dependent on quenching or blocking the quenching of CD's fluorescence. Static quenching, dynamic quenching, the inner filter effect (IFE), and other mechanisms may be involved in quenching the fluorescence of CDs [34]. The optical properties of CDs are greatly improved by doping them with heteroatoms such as phosphorus (P), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) [35–38]. CDs doped with sulfur and/or nitrogen are the most studied because of the close electronegativity of S and C, whereas the radii of N and C are the same [39,40]. Several techniques, including hydrothermal, chemical, and physical approaches, have been used to create N,S-CDs. The hydrothermal method is the most widely technique used for the preparation of N,S-CDs, but it is highly time-consuming and uses harmful reagents [36,41]. There is a high demand for ecofriendly, cost-effective, and efficient reagents to produce N,S-CDs to replace environmentally harmful ones. The microwave-assisted synthesis of N,S-CDs has attracted the attention of numerous researchers because of its time-saving benefits [42]. To overcome the low QY, lengthy high-temperature application, and difficulty of synthesizing CDs using the previously published methods, microwave irradiation was used [43]. This shortens the synthesis time and achieves simplicity, speed, greenness, and efficiency to produce CDs with high QY [44–48]. Recently, the green synthesis of CDs has garnered significant interest from researchers due to its numerous advantages, including environmental sustainability, the abundance of starting materials, affordability, and cost-effectiveness. Several green sources have also been used for the production of N,S-CDs, such as leaf extracts from plants such as Oscimum sanctum, Psidium guavaja, and Potamogeton pectinatus. Great efforts have been made to explore other green agents for the production of N,S-CDs [49–52].

Consequently, this work aims to develop a novel, environmentally friendly, and fast spectrofluorimetric method for RFX determination by using a novel microwave-assisted synthesis of N,S-CDs. For the first time, N,S-CDs were produced using thiourea as a nitrogen and sulfur source and watercress as a green source of carbon. The developed method relies on the ability of RFX to quench the native fluorescence of N,S-CDs via a static quenching mechanism and the inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence quenching was found to be proportional to RFX concentration. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study in which N,S-CDs were produced from watercress leaves. The developed method is quick, easy, and green and does not need complex procedures or costly reagents. Therefore, it is more appropriate for regular analysis of RFX.

Rifaximin (99.97 %, Optimus Pharma Pvt Limited, Telangana, India) and thiourea (Sigma‒Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) were utilized. Fresh watercress leaves were purchased from a local market. Britton–Robinson buffer (0.04 M, pH 2–12) was prepared by utilizing acetic acid glacial ACS (99.7 %, density = 1.05 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), boric acid glacial ACS, A.R. (99.5 %, density = 1.435 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), orthophosphoric acid (85 %, density = 1.88 g/mL; Ostim, Ankara, Turkey), and sodium hydroxide. Rifaxerose® film-coated tablets (Batch No. #221,390, labeled to contain 550 mg of rifaximin/tablet) were acquired from Egyptian Pharmacy.

The N,S-CDs were produced via a fresh microwave oven (25 KC, 900 Watt) model FMW, Fresh Co., Egypt. Fluorescence spectra were performed with a Cary Eclipse- Agilent spectrofluorimeter (Santa Clara, United States). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Jasco 6800, Japan) was used to characterize the synthesized N,S-CDs. The morphology of the synthesized N,S-CDs was examined via high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) at an accelerating voltage of 160 kV (JEOL, Tokyo). UV spectra were performed via a UV/VIS spectrophotometer (PG Instrument, UK). A zeta potential analyzer (Brookhaven Inst. Corp., BIC ZetaPlus) with distilled water was utilized to assess the zeta potential of the synthesized N,S-CDs. Moreover, an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer (JEOL, JSM-IT100LA, Japan) was utilized to analyze the N,S-CDs. A Jenway 3510 pH meter was utilized to adjust the solutions' pH. A digital analytical balance (Sartorius AG, Germany) and a cooling centrifuge (2–16 KL, Germany) were also utilized.

The watercress was bought from a local market and cleaned with tap water and then with distilled water. The N,S-CDs were prepared by mixing 2.0 g of thiourea and 2.5 g of freshly cleaned watercress leaves in a 250 mL beaker, which was then charred in a microwave for one minute. The product was cooled to room temperature before being dissolved and sonicated in 100 mL distilled water for five minutes. After that, the mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm, followed by filtration through filter paper to remove any insoluble material in the supernatant. Finally, the produced N,S-CDs were stored at 4 °C until further use (Scheme 1 ).

The QY of the N,S-CDs was determined via a single-point method. Quinine sulfate was the standard substance in this method; it was prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, and its QY was 0.54. The following equation was utilized to measure the QY:
(1)

where “ϕ”, “I”, “A”, “st”, and “η” are the QY, integrated fluorescence intensity of the sample, absorbance at the excitation wavelength, standard substance, and refractive index of the solvent, respectively, which is 1.33. The measured samples' absorbance values (Ast and AN,S-CDs) should be maintained under 0.1 to minimize the reabsorption effect.

A stock solution of RFX (1.0 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving 0.10 g of RFX in 20 % ethanol. To generate a 100.0 μg/mL RFX working solution, 10.0 mL of the 1.0 mg/mL RFX stock solution was removed, and 100.0 mL of distilled water was added to a volumetric flask. The prepared solutions were freshly prepared.

Under the optimal conditions, various aliquots of RFX working standard solution (100.0 µg/mL) ranging from 0.2–30.0 μg/mL were added to 70.0 μL of the N, S-CDs in a 5.0 mL volumetric flask. A volume of 1.5 mL Britton–Robinson buffer (pH=5) was then added, and the mixture was well mixed and brought to the mark with distilled water. All experiments were performed at room temperature, and the fluorescence intensities were measured immediately at 386 nm (λem) after excitation at 320 nm (λex). A blank solution was prepared, and its emission spectrum was measured simultaneously. The fluorescence quenching was plotted against RFX concentrations (µg/mL) to create the calibration curve, and the regression equation was then calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of RFX in pharmaceutical dosage form
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the dosage form, five Rifaxerose® tablets (labeled to contain 550 mg RFX/tablet) were weighed and then ground in a mortar. To prepare 1.0 mg/mL, a quantity equal to 0.18 g was sonicated with 20 mL of ethanol for 10 min and filtered into a 100 mL volumetric flask, after which the mixture was finally filled with distilled water. Various concentrations were prepared using 5.0 mL volumetric flasks through a series of dilutions using distilled water. Next, the reactions with N,S-CDs were carried out using the procedures described in Section ''2.6.''. The percentage recoveries from the tablets were then calculated.

In this study, a novel, eco-friendly, and quick method was applied for the preparation of N,S-CDs in just one minute via the microwave-assisted technique; watercress leaves were used as a green natural carbon source, and thiourea was used as a nitrogen and sulfur source (Scheme 1). When the N,S-CDs were exposed to optical light, they presented a bright yellow color, and when subjected to UV light, they presented strong blue fluorescence. Different microscopic and spectroscopic techniques, such as HR-TEM, FT-IR spectrometry, zeta potential determination, spectrofluorimetry, and UV‒visible spectrophotometry, were used to characterize the produced N,S-CDs.

The UV absorption spectrum of the N,S-CDs is shown in Fig. S1, where a clear UV absorption peak is located at 243 nm. Additionally, the fluorescence characteristics of the produced N,S-CDs were studied. The resulting fluorescence spectra of the CDs are presented in Fig. 1
a, where a prominent emission peak at 386 nm is visible after excitation at 320 nm. The N,S-CDs' emission was determined by the excitation wavelength (λex), which fell between 300 and 390 nm. The best excitation wavelength for maximum emission intensity was determined to be 320 nm (Fig. 1 b). The synthesized N,S-CDs demonstrated an acceptable quantum yield of 18.48 % when QS was used as a reference fluorescent substance. The reusability and high chemical stability of the N,S-CD were demonstrated by its constant fluorescence intensity over two months in the absence of any discernible precipitation.

Furthermore, HR-TEM was used to investigate the surface morphological characteristics and size of the N,S-CDs. The generated N,S-CDs were spherical and had particle sizes between 4 and 8 nm, with an average of 5.7 nm. This suggests that N,S-CDs have successfully formed (Fig. 2 ). After that, an FT-IR spectrometer was utilized to investigate the N,S-CDs' surface functional groups. Several stretching peaks were observed in the FT-IR spectra. These stretching peaks included N-H, O-H (3478 cm-1), C-H (3124 cm-1), C-N (2071 cm-1), C=C (1680 cm-1), C=S (1415 cm-1), and = C–H (664 cm-1) (Fig. 3
a) [53]. Moreover, a zeta potential analyzer was used to investigate the surface nature and electron density of the N,S-CDs. The synthesized N,S-CDs had a zeta potential value of -7.64 mV, confirming their negatively charged surface as a result of doping with electron-rich groups (a large number of oxygen-containing groups and N- and S-rich groups) (Fig. 3 b).

Elemental analysis was further conducted via an energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrometer to determine the compositions of the N- and S-CDs and verify the degree of doping with nitrogen and sulfur. According to Fig. 3 c, the main constituents of the N,S-CDs were carbon (54.98 %), nitrogen (34.43 %), oxygen (9.03 %), and sulfur (1.56 %). The findings show that a high degree of nitrogen and sulfur doping was attained.

In this study, N,S-CDs were prepared in a green manner and employed as fluorescent nanoprobes to determine RFX. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the developed fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was quantitatively quenched by the addition of increasing concentrations of RFX. Quenching occurs via three primary mechanisms, which include static quenching, dynamic quenching, and the inner filter effect (IFE) [36]. IFE is a quenching mechanism that occurs when the synthesized N,S-CDs' excitation spectrum and the quencher's absorption spectrum overlap [34]. The excitation spectra of the N and S-CDs and the absorption spectrum of RFX substantially overlapped, suggesting the possibility of an IFE, as shown in Fig. 4 b. To obtain further understanding of the quenching mechanism, Eq. (2) was used to correct the prepared N,S-CDs' fluorescence intensity for any possible IFE after increasing the concentration of RFX.

The observed and corrected fluorescence are represented by Fobs and Fcorr, respectively. The absorbance of RFX under study at the excitation and emission wavelengths is denoted by Aex and Aem. Additionally, the suppressed efficiency (%E) of the corrected and observed fluorescence was measured, as shown in equation # (3):

where % E, F0, and F are the suppression efficiency, the blank's fluorescence intensity, and Fcorr or Fobs, respectively. The correlation between the concentrations (μg/mL) of the drug and both the corrected and observed N,S-CDs fluorescence intensities (%E) (Fig. 4 c) confirmed that the IFE plays a critical role in the RFX-induced quenching of N,S-CD fluorescence.

The Stern-Volmer equation was also utilized to investigate additional mechanisms, such as static or dynamic quenching, as shown in Eq. (4):
(4)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K sv
[ Q ]
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
where F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of N and S-CDs at λex/λem wavelengths of 320 nm and 386 nm before and after adding the drug; The Stern-Volmer constant is denoted by ksv, the fluorescence quenching constant by kq; The average lifetime (10–8 s) is represented by τ0; and the molar concentration of RFX is indicated by [Q]. The developed method was applied at various temperatures (293, 303, and 313 K) to investigate the quenching mechanisms that might accompany the IFE. In dynamic quenching, the KSV constant enhances with increasing temperature, but in static quenching, it decreases as temperature rises [54]. The values of Ksv were found to be 2.7 × 10–2, 2.1 × 10–2, and 1.1 × 10–2. As shown in Fig. 4 d, the Ksv values decrease as the temperature increases, suggesting that the mechanism of quenching with RFX is static quenching. Moreover, the values of Kq were calculated and found to be 2.8 × 1012, 2.1 × 1012, and 1.1 × 1012, which further proves the static quenching mechanism. Accordingly, the mechanism of quenching involves both IFE and static quenching.

Various factors that could influence the preparation of N,S-CDs were studied, such as the amount of thiourea, the amount of watercress leaves, and the time of synthesis needed to obtain the highest fluorescence intensity at 386 nm. First, the effect of the amount of thiourea was studied (0.5–3.5 g). As the amount of thiourea increased, the intensity of the fluorescence at 386 nm increased stepwise up to 2.5 gm. When the amount of thiourea was increased to 3.5 gm, the fluorescence intensity decreased. Therefore, 2.5 gm of thiourea was chosen as the optimum amount (Fig. S2a). After that, the effect of the amount of watercress leaves was studied over the range of 0.5–3.0 g. Larger amounts of watercress leaves resulted in incomplete charring inside the microwave, thus causing the incompleteness of the reaction. When the amount of watercress leaves increased, the resulting fluorescence at 386 nm increased to 2.0 gm and then decreased at greater than 2.0 gm. Thus, 2.0 gm of watercress leaves was selected as the optimum amount (Fig. S2b). Finally, the effect of the duration of the synthesis was studied over the range of 10 s to 90 s, as this duration is the most important factor affecting the production of N,S-CDs. The emission intensity enhanced with time up to 60 s; following that, a relatively constant trend was noted (Fig. S2c). Consequently, the N,S-CDs were prepared in just 60 s. These findings imply that the process of forming N,S-CDs is time-dependent and eventually reaches a steady state after a definite time [55].

Numerous variables that could influence the reaction were optimized, including the volume of N,S-CDs, the pH of the buffer, the volume of the buffer, and the incubation time. The fluorescence intensity at 386 nm was determined in both the absence and presence of RFX. To achieve the highest possible sensitivity, optimization was done to obtain the highest difference (F0-F) in the presence of RFX.

The effects of N,S-CDs volume was investigated from 10.0 to 90.0 µL (Fig. S3a). The fluorescence quenching (F0-F) was observed to increase significantly from 10.0 to 40.0 µL, followed by a slight increase over the range of 40.0–50.0 µL. A further significant increase in (F0-F) was observed upon increasing the volume of N,S-CDs from 50.0 to 60.0 µL, which was followed by a plateau. The volume of 70.0 µL of N,S-CDs was selected to be the optimum because of the stable and high fluorescence intensity.

Monitoring the pH effect is crucial because it may impact the rate and yield of the reaction. The pH effect on the reaction was investigated by utilizing Briton Robinson buffer at various pH values between 3.0 and 9.0. The greatest intensity was found in the pH between 4.0 and 6.0, with the highest fluorescence quenching (F0-F) occurring at pH 5.0 (Fig. S3b). The reduced intensity at low and high pH values may be due to the instability of RFX in highly acidic and basic media, as it has pKa values of 3.66 and 11.87 [14]. So, pH 5.0 was determined as the optimum pH.

The effect of buffer volume was investigated over a range of 500.0–3000.0 µL, and it was found that 1500.0 µL of buffer produced the greatest quenching (Fig. S3c). As a result, 1500.0 µL of Britton–Robinson buffer at pH 5 was used.

The incubation time was studied over 60 min, and no change in the fluorescence response was observed. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity was determined instantaneously after adding the drug to N,S-CDs (Fig. S3d).

The proposed method was thoroughly validated according to ICH guidelines. Many factors, such as linearity, range, precision, accuracy, limits of detection and quantitation, selectivity, as well as robustness, were considered.

The linear range was evaluated by graphing the fluorescence quenching (F0-F) versus the concentration of RFX within the range of 0.2–30.0 µg/mL. The following equation was obtained after applying linear regression analysis:

r = 0.9996
)
where C is the RFX concentration (µg/mL).
The correlation coefficient (r) was 0.9996, indicating that the developed method was adequately linear. The analytical validation results are provided in Table 1 .

To evaluate the accuracy of the proposed approach, three different concentrations (5.0, 10.0, and 15.0 µg/mL) over the linear range were studied. Each concentration was measured in triplicate, and the recovery percentage was determined. The accuracy of the method is revealed in Table S1, which shows that the percentage recoveries are between 98 % and 102 %. The precision of the proposed method was investigated by evaluating the intra- and interday precision. For intraday precision, the developed method was used to determine three replicates of three distinct concentrations within the designated range on the same day. To study the interday precision, the same steps were carried out three times on three consecutive days. The minimal percent relative standard deviation values (< 2.0 %) indicate the precision of the proposed method (Table S2).

Both the LOD and LOQ were measured according to the ICH guideline Q2 (R1), relying on the standard deviation of the blank samples (σ) and the slope of the calibration curve (b) via the following equations:
(6)
LOD = 3.3
*
σ
b
(7)
LOQ = 10
*
σ
b
The determined LODs and LOQs of 0.06 and 0.19 μg/mL, demonstrated the high sensitivity of the proposed method.

Following the guidelines provided by the ICH, when there is no interference from interfering ingredients, the method is selective. The selectivity of the developed method for RFX estimation was studied, and the results revealed that there was no interference from the excipients and that RFX could be accurately detected in the tablet, with good mean % recovery (99.8 %) and small % RSD values (less than 0.97 %). Substances such as lactose, mannitol, maltose, starch, dextrin, citric acid, Ca+2, Na+, Mg+2, Ba+2, and pb+2, which can be present concurrently with RFX in its pharmaceutical dosage forms, were carefully investigated to eliminate any potential interference from the tablet excipients. These substances were used in excess concentrations, approximately 10 times higher than the concentration of RFX (20.0 μg/mL). According to the results shown in Fig. S4, no noticeable fluorescence quenching was induced by the studied interfering substances. These results imply that the developed method is very selective for RFX even when substances that may coexist are present.

The robustness of the developed method was assessed by making small changes to the optimization parameters, such as the volume of N and S-CDs (70.0 μL ± 5), Britton–Robinson buffer pH (5 ± 0.3), and volume (1.5 mL ± 0.3). To estimate the effect of each factor, only a single factor was altered while keeping the other parameters at their optimal values. The percentage recovery, standard deviation (SD), and %RSD were determined based on the calculations presented in Table S3. The method's robustness is indicated by good recovery percentage values and low %RSD values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the developed method to pharmaceutical tablets (Rifaxerose® tablets)
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed method was successfully applied to determine RFX in its pharmaceutical tablets (Rifaxerose® film-coated tablets), with small %RSD values (≤0.97 %) and high %recoveries (98.2–100.9 %). Student's t-test and variance ratio F-test were utilized to compare the results of the proposed method with those of the published HPLC/UV method [16]. Accuracy was evaluated by comparing the average percentage recovery (t-test), and precision was evaluated by comparing variances (F-test) [56]. Based on statistical tables at the appropriate degrees of freedom, theoretical values at the 95 % confidence level (P=0.05) were determined for every test. According to the assay results listed in Table 2 , there was no statistically significant difference between the reported HPLC/UV method and the developed spectrofluorometric method. Nevertheless, our suggested method is simpler, quicker, less expensive, and environmentally friendly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Greenness assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous metrics have been developed to evaluate the environmental effect of different analytical methods as a result of an increasing effort to produce more environmentally friendly analytical techniques. These measures and technologies can help chemists find more sustainable procedures, even if each one has unique calculations and requirements [57,58]. The greenness of the developed approach was assessed via different tools, such as the analytical Eco-scale [59,60], the Analytical GREEnness (AGREE) [61], and the Modified Green Analytical Procedure Index (MoGAPI) [62]. The analytical eco-scale is a semiquantitative tool used to assess the greenness of analytical procedures. The methodology for this scale involves subtracting penalty points (PPs) from a maximum score of 100 for each parameter that fails to adhere to the ideal green analysis [59,60]. Table 3 shows the excellent results of our developed method, with a higher score (91) than the results of the published comparison methods [16]. Moreover, AGREE is a metric tool that is utilized to assess the twelve important factors to determine the environmental and occupational hazards associated with the analytical method. The range of scores for AGREE is 0–1. A method is considered greener if its score is closer to 1. A novel MoGAPI tool was also utilized to evaluate the greenness of the developed method. The MoGAPI tool was recently reported by Mansour et al in 2024 [62]. MoGAPI is another improved version of GAPI [63] which includes five pentagrams with three colors (green, yellow, and red) to assess the greenness of the following elements: sample preparation, reagents and solvents, instrumentation, and quantification. The MoGAPI presents a modified tool that combines the visual design of GAPI with accurate total scores. MoGAPI offers an extensive assessment of the eco-friendliness of methods, enabling more precise and impartial method comparisons. The developed approach uses watercress as a natural source of carbon to synthesize N,S-CDs via a microwave-assisted method. Additionally, distilled water was used along with buffer for the direct measurement of RFX. The method's high AGREE score of 0.80 and green-colored MoGAPI with a score of 82, as displayed in Fig. 5 , indicate that it has an excellent greenness level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Blueness assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: The Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) is a newly created tool that provides a quantitative framework for determining the “blueness” or applicability of analytical procedures. It provided more information to help understand the economic and practical factors. This tool presents an asteroid pictogram containing a graphical representation of the data with a total score at the center. The composite BAGI score is generated by taking the geometric mean of the values for 10 parameters during the analysis procedure. A higher BAGI score indicates more applicability, usefulness, and fit for its intended usage. The pictogram features multiple blue color grades (dark blue, blue, and light blue represent high to poor compliance, with white indicating no compliance at all) [64]. Our developed method obtains a BAGI score of 72.5. The findings of the BAGI evaluation demonstrate that our system has a lot to offer in terms of overall functioning, danger minimization, and time and cost savings, as presented in Fig. 5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Whiteness assessment of the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: Researchers may conduct a more thorough analysis, focusing on the extent of sustainability concerns. This necessitates a detailed analysis of the areas that must be strengthened or diminished and a practical assessment of the approach. The newly developed White Analytical Chemistry is used to evaluate the quality of the analytical method in terms of time, cost, validation efficiency, and equipment handling proficiency. To assess whiteness, the RGB12 algorithm uses three colors: red, green, and blue, which stand for analytical, greenness principles, as well as practicality [65]. The sum of the scores for the three regions is the whiteness percentage. The suggested spectrofluorimetric method has a high score of 98.8 when considering the red region. The method received a high score of 96.5 in the blue region and a score of 94.2 in the green region. Thus, as shown in Fig. S5, the suggested approach was white, fitted to the intended application, and had a score of 96.5.

The proposed method was compared with different methods, including spectrophotometric [7–9], spectrofluorimetric [10], infrared spectroscopic [11], electrochemistry [12], capillary electrophoresis [13], and chromatographic methods [14–17], which have been reported for RFX determination in various samples. The literature provides a limited number of drug-determination methods. Another gap is the absence of ecologically friendly methods that produce the least amount of hazardous waste. These methods also suffer from many drawbacks, such as low sensitivity [7–9], long processing times [10], several steps [12], complicated processes [13], and the use of toxic solvents [15–17]. Compared with these published methods, the developed spectrofluorimetric method is simpler, quicker, and does not require catalysis or heating because of the high yield and instantaneous reaction. With respect to the developed method, Table 4 shows that the produced N,S-CDs were able to determine RFX with higher sensitivity and lower LOD than the previously published methods. Although the other published methods [15–17] are more sensitive than the developed method; they take a lot of time, are less eco-friendly, and require complicated conditions and synthesis. Consequently, the developed method outperforms the other published methods and has sufficient sensitivity for regular RFX analysis in quality control labs and pharmaceutical companies.

For the first time, a novel ultrafast microwave-assisted synthetic method was performed for the production of N,S-CDs in just one minute using thiourea as a nitrogen and sulfur source and watercress as a green carbon source. The N,S-CDs were characterized via different spectroscopic and microscopic techniques. The synthesized N,S-CDs were further utilized as fluorescent nanosensors to determine the pharmaceutical dosage of RFX. The developed method was validated following the ICHQ2 (R1) guidelines. The developed nanosensor demonstrated good and wide linearity between 0.2 and 30.0 µg/mL, with a low percentage RSD (≤ 0.97 %) and high percentage recoveries (98.2–100.9 %). Following the greenness (MoGAPI, AGREE, and analytical eco-scale), the blueness (BAGI), and the whiteness (RGB12) assessment metrics, the developed spectrofluorimetric method was proven to be more environmentally friendly in comparison to the published methods. Moreover, this work represents the first analytical method to explain the application of N,S-CDs for determining RFX in tablets.
